modules.title: Basic Concepts of Data Structures
topics: Performance Analysis — Time and Space Complexity

notes.detailed: Data Structures are ways of organizing data so that it can be used efficiently. The System Life Cycle covers requirements, analysis, design, coding, verification, and maintenance. An algorithm must be finite, definite, input/output-driven, and effective. Time complexity measures the number of steps as a function of input size. Space complexity measures memory usage. Big-O notation gives the upper bound: if f(n)=O(g(n)), then f grows no faster than g. A Stack is a LIFO structure supporting push, pop, peek, and isEmpty in O(1). A Queue is FIFO; circular queues solve the false-overflow problem by wrapping the rear pointer. A Priority Queue dequeues the highest-priority element first, used in schedulers and Dijkstra's algorithm.
short_notes: Algorithm properties: Finiteness, Definiteness, Input, Output, Effectiveness
Big-O (upper bound), Omega (lower bound), Theta (tight bound)
Stack: LIFO — push/pop O(1), used in function calls, expression evaluation
Queue: FIFO — enqueue/dequeue O(1), used in BFS, scheduling
Circular Queue: fixes false overflow — rear = (rear+1) % MAX
Priority Queue: elements dequeued by priority, not arrival order
Deque (Double Ended Queue): insert/delete at both ends

important_topics: Circular Queue logic and overflow conditions
Infix to Postfix conversion using stacks
expected_questions: Calculate the frequency count and time complexity of a given code segment.
Explain the differences between a circular queue and a normal queue.
Write an algorithm to convert an infix expression to postfix using a stack.
Compare Big-O, Omega, and Theta notations with examples.
Implement a priority queue using an array and explain its operations.

videos.title: Big O Notation — Time and Space Complexity

modules.title: Arrays and Searching

notes.detailed: Arrays store elements of the same type in contiguous memory, enabling O(1) random access. Polynomials can be stored as coefficient-exponent pairs in an array. Sparse matrices (mostly zeros) are efficiently stored using a triplet format (row, col, value) or linked lists to save space. Linear Search scans each element sequentially — O(n) worst case, suitable for unsorted data. Binary Search requires a sorted array; it repeatedly halves the search space — O(log n) time. At each step, compare mid = (low+high)/2 with target; adjust low or high accordingly.
short_notes: Array: O(1) access, O(n) insertion/deletion
Polynomial: stored as (coefficient, exponent) pairs
Sparse Matrix: use triplet (row, col, value) to save space
Linear Search: O(n) — works on unsorted data
Binary Search: O(log n) — requires sorted array
Binary Search: mid = (low + high) / 2

expected_questions: Write algorithms for Linear Search and Binary Search and compare their time complexities.
Represent the given sparse matrix in triplet form and write addition algorithm.
Write an algorithm to add two polynomials stored as arrays.

modules.title: Linked List and Memory Management
topics: Singly Linked List — Insertion, Deletion, Search

notes.detailed: A Linked List is a dynamic data structure where each node holds data and a pointer to the next node. Singly linked lists support insertion and deletion in O(1) at head, O(n) at arbitrary position. Doubly linked lists allow traversal in both directions with prev and next pointers. Circular linked lists connect the last node back to the first, useful for round-robin scheduling. Stacks and Queues implemented with linked lists have no overflow unless memory is exhausted. Polynomials linked list nodes store (coeff, exp, next). Memory management involves malloc/free; garbage collection reclaims unreachable nodes. Boundary tag method tracks allocated/free blocks.
short_notes: Linked List: dynamic size, no contiguous memory needed
Singly LL: O(1) head insert, O(n) search/delete
Doubly LL: bidirectional traversal, prev + next pointers
Circular LL: last node points to head — used in round-robin
LL Stack: push/pop at head — O(1), no overflow
Garbage collection: reclaims memory of unreachable nodes

important_topics: Singly Linked List all operations with algorithms
Comparing array vs linked list for stack/queue
expected_questions: Write algorithms for insertion and deletion in a singly linked list.
Compare arrays and linked lists for implementing stacks and queues.
Write an algorithm to add two polynomials represented as linked lists.
Explain doubly linked list with all operations.
What is garbage collection? Explain boundary tag method.

videos.title: Stack and Queue using Linked List

modules.title: Trees and Graphs
topics: Binary Search Tree — Insert, Delete, Search

notes.detailed: A tree is a connected acyclic graph. A binary tree has at most two children per node. Binary trees are represented as arrays (for complete trees: left child at 2i, right at 2i+1) or as linked nodes. Inorder (Left-Root-Right) gives sorted output for BST. Preorder (Root-Left-Right) is used for tree copying. Postorder (Left-Right-Root) is used for deletion. BST property: left subtree < root < right subtree; search and insert O(h) where h is height. Graphs are represented using adjacency matrix (O(V²) space) or adjacency list (O(V+E) space). DFS uses a stack and explores as far as possible before backtracking — O(V+E). BFS uses a queue and explores level by level — O(V+E). Applications include shortest paths, topological sort, spanning trees.
short_notes: Binary Tree: at most 2 children; height = log2(n) for complete tree
Inorder = Left-Root-Right → sorted output for BST
Preorder = Root-Left-Right → tree copy/serialization
Postorder = Left-Right-Root → tree deletion
BST: Insert/Search O(log n) avg, O(n) worst
DFS: Stack-based, O(V+E)
BFS: Queue-based, O(V+E), finds shortest path (unweighted)

important_topics: BST insert, delete (all cases), and search
DFS and BFS with adjacency list/matrix
expected_questions: Find the Depth First Search and Breadth First Search of a given graph.
List the properties of Binary Search Tree. Write an algorithm to search an element from a BST.
Write an iterative algorithm for inorder traversal of a Binary Tree.
Construct a BST from the given list of elements and trace a deletion operation.
Compare adjacency matrix and adjacency list representations of graphs.

videos.title: Graph Representation — Adjacency Matrix and List

modules.title: Sorting and Hashing

notes.detailed: Sorting arranges elements in order. Selection Sort finds the minimum and places it at the start — O(n²) always. Insertion Sort builds a sorted array incrementally — O(n²) worst, O(n) best. Merge Sort divides, recursively sorts, then merges — O(n log n) always, O(n) space. Quick Sort partitions around a pivot — O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst; in-place. Heap Sort uses a max-heap; build heap O(n), sort O(n log n). Hashing maps keys to indices via a hash function for O(1) average lookup. Division method: h(k) = k mod m. Mid-square: square the key, extract middle digits. Collisions resolved by chaining (linked lists at buckets) or open addressing (linear probing, quadratic probing, double hashing).
short_notes: Selection Sort: O(n²) — finds min each pass
Insertion Sort: O(n²) worst, O(n) best — good for nearly sorted
Merge Sort: O(n log n) all cases — stable, needs O(n) space
Quick Sort: O(n log n) avg, O(n²) worst — in-place
Heap Sort: O(n log n) — uses max-heap
Hash function: h(k) = k mod m (Division method)
Collision: Chaining (linked list) or Open Addressing (probing)

important_topics: Trace Merge Sort and Quick Sort on given arrays
Comparison of all sorting algorithms by complexity and stability
expected_questions: Trace the execution of Quick Sort and Merge Sort on a given array.
Compare sorting algorithms with respect to time complexity, space complexity, and stability.
Explain hashing with the division method. How is collision handled using chaining?
Write the algorithm for Heap Sort and trace it on an example.
What is open addressing? Compare linear probing, quadratic probing, and double hashing.

trend_analysis.exam_pattern: Part A: 10 × 3 marks; Part B: 5 modules × 14 marks (choose 1 of 2)

modules.title: Basic Structure of Computers

notes.detailed: A computer has five functional units: Input, Memory, ALU, Control Unit, and Output. The CPU fetches instructions from memory, decodes them, and executes them — the fetch-decode-execute cycle. Memory is byte-addressable; each location holds one byte. Big-endian stores MSB at the lowest address; little-endian stores LSB first. A bus is a shared communication path; the system bus has address, data, and control lines. Addressing modes include: immediate (operand in instruction), register (operand in register), direct (address in instruction), indirect (address points to address), indexed, and relative.
short_notes: Five units: Input, Memory, ALU, CU, Output
Fetch-Decode-Execute: basic CPU instruction cycle
Big-endian: MSB first; Little-endian: LSB first
System bus: Address bus + Data bus + Control bus
Immediate mode: fastest, operand in instruction itself
Indirect mode: double memory access, most flexible

expected_questions: Explain all addressing modes with examples.
Give the significance of the instruction cycle.
Distinguish between big endian and little endian notations.
Explain the functional units of a computer with a block diagram.

modules.title: Register Transfer Logic and Processor Logic Design
topics: Register Transfer, Bus and Memory Transfers

notes.detailed: Register Transfer Language (RTL) describes micro-operations symbolically. A micro-operation is an elementary operation on data in registers. Types include: arithmetic (add, subtract, increment), logic (AND, OR, NOT, XOR), and shift (logical, arithmetic, circular). The ALU performs all arithmetic and logic operations. The accumulator is a register used to hold intermediate results. The instruction register (IR) holds the current instruction; the program counter (PC) holds the address of the next instruction.
short_notes: RTL: symbolic notation for register transfers — e.g., R1 ← R2
Micro-operations: arithmetic, logic, shift
PC: holds next instruction address
IR: holds current instruction
MAR: Memory Address Register
MDR: Memory Data Register

expected_questions: Design an ALU that performs arithmetic and logic operations.
Explain arithmetic and logic micro-operations with examples.
What is Register Transfer Language? Give examples.

videos.title: Flip Flops — SR, JK, D, T

modules.title: Arithmetic Algorithms and Pipelining
topics: Addition and Subtraction — Signed Magnitude, 2's Complement

notes.detailed: 2's complement is standard for signed integers. Addition is done directly; subtraction uses the 2's complement of the subtrahend. Booth's algorithm multiplies signed binary numbers efficiently by examining pairs of bits, replacing groups of 1s with +1 and −1 operations. Pipelining divides instruction execution into stages (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB) so multiple instructions overlap. A k-stage pipeline can have k instructions in flight simultaneously, achieving throughput of 1 instruction/clock in steady state. Hazards: structural (resource conflict), data (RAW, WAR, WAW), and control (branches).
short_notes: 2's complement: invert all bits + 1
Booth's algorithm: handles signed multiplication efficiently
Pipeline stages: IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB
Pipeline speedup = n × k / (k + n - 1) ≈ k for large n
Hazards: Structural, Data (RAW/WAR/WAW), Control

expected_questions: Trace Booth's Algorithm for given operands.
Explain the different types of pipeline hazards and their solutions.
What is an instruction pipeline? Explain the five-stage pipeline with a diagram.

modules.title: Control Logic Design

notes.detailed: The control unit generates signals to coordinate all CPU components. Hardwired control uses combinational logic for speed — fast but inflexible. Micro-programmed control stores control signals as microinstructions in a control memory (ROM) — flexible, easier to modify. A microprogram is a sequence of microinstructions. The micro-PC sequences through the control store. Horizontal micro-programming: one bit per control signal (fast, wide word). Vertical micro-programming: encoded signals (compact, needs decoder).
short_notes: Hardwired: fast, inflexible, uses combinational logic
Microprogrammed: flexible, uses control ROM
Horizontal microcode: one bit per signal — fast but wide
Vertical microcode: encoded — compact but needs decoder
Control memory: ROM storing microinstructions

expected_questions: Compare hardwired and micro-programmed control units.
Explain horizontal and vertical micro-programming.
What is a micro-program? Describe address sequencing in micro-programmed control.

modules.title: Basic Processing Units, I/O and Memory
topics: Cache Memory — Mapping Functions (Direct, Associative, Set-Associative)

notes.detailed: I/O can be memory-mapped (I/O registers in memory address space, same instructions as memory) or I/O-mapped (separate address space, IN/OUT instructions). Interrupts allow devices to signal the CPU asynchronously. DMA transfers data directly between memory and I/O without CPU intervention, using a DMA controller that takes bus control. Cache memory sits between CPU and RAM; it exploits temporal and spatial locality. Direct mapping: block i maps to cache line i mod N. Fully associative: any block maps anywhere. Set-associative: compromise. Replacement policies: LRU, FIFO, Random.
short_notes: Memory-mapped I/O: I/O uses memory addresses
Interrupt: asynchronous CPU notification from device
DMA: bulk data transfer without CPU, uses bus mastering
Cache: fast SRAM between CPU and RAM
Direct mapping: one possible cache line per block
Fully associative: any block → any line (most flexible)
LRU replacement: evict least recently used block

expected_questions: Compare I/O mapped I/O and memory mapped I/O.
Explain the three cache mapping functions with examples.
What is DMA? Explain how DMA transfers data.
Explain the interrupt mechanism with an example.
Compare SRAM and DRAM memory technologies.

videos.title: Memory Mapped I/O vs I/O Mapped I/O

modules.title: Introduction and Entity Relationship Model
topics: Database System Architecture — Levels of Abstraction

notes.detailed: A DBMS is software managing an organized collection of data providing efficient, reliable access. The three-schema architecture has External (user views), Conceptual (logical structure), and Internal (physical storage) levels. The ER model represents data as entities and relationships. An entity is a real-world object; attributes describe it. A key attribute uniquely identifies an entity. Relationships have cardinality: one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many. Weak entities lack a key attribute and depend on a strong entity (owner). Participation: total (double line) = every entity participates; partial (single line) = some participate.
short_notes: Three-schema: External → Conceptual → Internal
Entity: real-world object; Attribute: property of entity
Primary key: uniquely identifies each entity instance
Cardinality: 1:1, 1:N, M:N
Weak entity: no key, depends on owner entity
Total participation: double line in ER diagram

important_topics: ER diagram construction for given scenarios
expected_questions: Design an ER diagram for a given scenario (e.g., Hospital, Library, University).
Explain the different types of attributes in the ER model with examples.
What are weak entities? Give an example and show it in an ER diagram.
Compare total and partial participation with examples.

modules.title: Relational Model and SQL DDL
topics: Relational Model: Concepts — Relation, Tuple, Attribute, Domain
Relational Algebra: Select, Project, Cartesian Product
Relational Algebra: Join — Equi-join, Natural join, Outer join
Converting ER Diagram to Relational Schema
Integrity Constraints: PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, NOT NULL, UNIQUE

notes.detailed: The relational model represents data as tables (relations). A tuple is a row; an attribute is a column; a domain is the set of permissible values. Relational algebra: SELECT (σ) filters rows by condition; PROJECT (π) selects columns; CARTESIAN PRODUCT (×) combines all tuple pairs. JOIN combines tables on a condition: equi-join uses = operator; natural join eliminates duplicate columns; outer joins (left, right, full) preserve non-matching tuples. SQL DDL creates schema: CREATE TABLE with constraints. Integrity constraints enforce correctness: PRIMARY KEY uniquely identifies rows, FOREIGN KEY enforces referential integrity.
short_notes: Relation = Table; Tuple = Row; Attribute = Column
σ (Select): filter rows — σ condition (R)
π (Project): select columns — π attrs (R)
Natural Join: joins on common attributes, removes duplicates
Left Outer Join: keeps all left tuples, nulls for unmatched right
CREATE TABLE defines schema + constraints
FOREIGN KEY: references primary key of another table

expected_questions: Synthesize the given ER diagram into a relational schema.
Write relational algebra expressions for given queries.
Write SQL DDL statements to create tables with appropriate constraints.
Differentiate equi-join, natural join, and outer join with examples.

videos.title: Converting ER Diagram to Relational Schema

modules.title: SQL DML and Physical Data Organization
topics: SQL DML: SELECT — Single and Multi-table Queries
Aggregate Functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX
Physical Data Organization: Files, Records, Blocking Factor

notes.detailed: SQL SELECT syntax: SELECT columns FROM tables WHERE condition GROUP BY col HAVING condition ORDER BY col. Aggregate functions operate on groups: COUNT(*), SUM(col), AVG(col). Subqueries embed one SELECT inside another; correlated subqueries reference the outer query. Views are virtual tables defined by queries; they simplify complex queries and provide security. Triggers are procedural code that execute on INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE events. Physical organization affects I/O performance. Blocking factor = block size / record size. A B+ tree index stores data only in leaves; internal nodes are used for navigation. All leaves linked for range queries.
short_notes: SELECT-FROM-WHERE-GROUP BY-HAVING-ORDER BY
HAVING filters groups; WHERE filters rows
Correlated subquery: references outer query column
View: virtual table, no physical storage
Trigger: fires on INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE
B+ Tree: data in leaves, internal nodes for routing
Blocking factor = floor(block_size / record_size)

important_topics: Complex SQL queries with joins and aggregates
expected_questions: Write SQL queries for given problem statements involving joins, aggregates, and subqueries.
Explain B+ tree indexing with an example.
What are views? How are they created and what are their advantages?
What are triggers? Write a trigger for a given scenario.
Explain the different types of file organization for physical data storage.

videos.title: SQL Aggregate Functions and GROUP BY

modules.title: Normalization

notes.detailed: Normalization eliminates redundancy and anomalies (insertion, deletion, update). A functional dependency X → Y means X determines Y. Armstrong's Axioms: Reflexivity, Augmentation, Transitivity. 1NF: all attributes atomic (no repeating groups). 2NF: 1NF + no partial dependency (non-key attribute depends on entire primary key). 3NF: 2NF + no transitive dependency (non-key attribute depends on another non-key attribute). BCNF: every determinant is a candidate key — stronger than 3NF. A decomposition is lossless if the original relation can be reconstructed. It is dependency-preserving if all FDs are preserved.
short_notes: FD: X → Y means X determines Y uniquely
Armstrong's Axioms: Reflexivity, Augmentation, Transitivity
1NF: atomic values only
2NF: 1NF + no partial dependency on primary key
3NF: 2NF + no transitive dependency
BCNF: every determinant is a candidate key
Lossless decomposition: R1 ∩ R2 → R1 or R1 ∩ R2 → R2

expected_questions: Normalize a given relation to 3NF and BCNF showing each step.
Find all candidate keys for a given relation with functional dependencies.
Explain the three types of anomalies that normalization addresses.
Distinguish between 3NF and BCNF with examples.
What is lossless decomposition? How do you test for it?

modules.title: Transactions, Concurrency and Recovery
topics: Concurrency Control: Lost Update, Dirty Read
Lock-based Protocols: Shared and Exclusive Locks

notes.detailed: A transaction is a logical unit of work. ACID properties: Atomicity (all or nothing), Consistency (DB constraints maintained), Isolation (concurrent transactions appear serial), Durability (committed changes survive failure). States: Active → Partially Committed → Committed / Failed → Aborted. The system log records all changes for recovery. Concurrency problems: lost update (two transactions overwrite each other), dirty read (reading uncommitted data). Two-Phase Locking (2PL): growing phase (acquire locks), shrinking phase (release locks) — ensures serializability. Recovery uses UNDO (uncommitted transactions) and REDO (committed but not yet written to disk).
short_notes: ACID: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability
Dirty Read: reading uncommitted data from another transaction
Lost Update: two transactions overwrite each other's changes
2PL: growing phase (acquire) then shrinking phase (release)
UNDO: rollback uncommitted changes on failure
REDO: reapply committed changes not yet written to disk
Checkpoint: periodic disk flush to limit redo scope

expected_questions: Explain ACID properties of transactions with examples.
What is the Two-Phase Locking protocol? How does it ensure serializability?
Describe the transaction state diagram.
Explain the UNDO/REDO recovery technique.
What are the concurrency problems in DBMS? How are they solved?

videos.title: Database Recovery Techniques — UNDO and REDO

modules.title: Introduction to Operating Systems
topics: Types of OS: Batch, Time-sharing, Real-time, Distributed

notes.detailed: An OS is system software managing hardware resources and providing services to programs. Goals: convenience and efficiency. System calls are the programming interface to OS services (e.g., fork, exec, read, write). OS structures: Monolithic (Linux) — all kernel code in one layer, fast but fragile; Layered — each layer uses only lower layers; Microkernel (Mach) — minimal kernel, services in user space, more stable. A process is a program in execution with its own PCB storing: PID, state, program counter, registers, memory info, I/O info. States: New → Ready → Running → Waiting → Terminated. Context switch saves the running process's state and loads another's.
short_notes: OS: manages CPU, memory, I/O, files
System call: interface between user programs and kernel
Monolithic: fast, all in kernel space (Linux)
Microkernel: minimal kernel, services in user space (Mach)
PCB: PID, state, PC, registers, memory maps
Context switch: save old process state, load new — overhead

expected_questions: Explain the process state transition diagram.
What is a system call? How does hardware find the OS kernel after system switch-on?
Why is context switching considered an overhead?
Compare monolithic and microkernel OS architectures.

modules.title: Process Scheduling

notes.detailed: CPU scheduling determines which process runs next. Criteria: CPU utilization (maximize), throughput (maximize), turnaround time (minimize), waiting time (minimize), response time (minimize). FCFS: non-preemptive, simple, suffers convoy effect. SJF: optimal avg waiting time, may cause starvation; preemptive version = SRTF. Priority: starvation solved by aging. Round Robin: preemptive with time quantum q; good response time; if q too large = FCFS, too small = too many context switches. Multilevel feedback queue is the most general algorithm.
short_notes: FCFS: simple, non-preemptive, convoy effect
SJF: optimal waiting time, starvation of long jobs
SRTF: preemptive SJF
Priority: aging prevents starvation
Round Robin: preemptive, time quantum q, best response time
Turnaround time = completion - arrival time
Waiting time = turnaround - burst time

important_topics: Gantt chart and calculation for all scheduling algorithms
expected_questions: Given processes with arrival and burst times, compute Gantt charts and average waiting time for FCFS, SJF, and Round Robin.
Explain the convoy effect in FCFS scheduling.
Compare preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling with examples.
What are the criteria for comparing CPU scheduling algorithms?

modules.title: Process Synchronization
topics: Requirements: Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting
Classic Problems: Dining Philosophers, Readers-Writers, Bounded Buffer

notes.detailed: The critical section is code that accesses shared resources. Requirements for a correct solution: Mutual Exclusion (only one process at a time), Progress (if no process is in CS, a waiting process must eventually enter), Bounded Waiting (limit on how many times others can enter before a waiting process). Peterson's solution uses two variables (flag[], turn) for two processes. A semaphore is an integer variable with atomic wait() (P) and signal() (V) operations. Binary semaphore = mutex. Counting semaphore manages N resources. Dining Philosophers: 5 philosophers, 5 forks — risk of deadlock; solution: one philosopher picks right fork first.
short_notes: Critical section: code accessing shared resource
Mutual exclusion: only one process in CS at a time
Semaphore: wait() = P (decrement), signal() = V (increment)
Binary semaphore = mutex lock
Counting semaphore: tracks N available resources
Dining Philosophers: deadlock risk, solved by asymmetric solution
Monitor: high-level sync construct with condition variables

expected_questions: What is the critical section problem? State its requirements.
Explain Peterson's solution for the critical section problem.
Solve the Bounded Buffer problem using semaphores.
What is a monitor? How does it differ from a semaphore?
Explain the Readers-Writers problem and its solution.

modules.title: Deadlocks
topics: Deadlock Conditions: Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait

notes.detailed: Deadlock occurs when processes wait for each other in a circular chain. Four necessary conditions (Coffman): Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait. Resource Allocation Graph: circles = processes, rectangles = resources; cycle = possible deadlock (definite if one instance per resource). Prevention: eliminate one condition. Avoidance: Banker's Algorithm maintains a safe state. Safety algorithm: find a process whose need ≤ available resources, run it, release resources, repeat. Detection: run detection algorithm periodically. Recovery: kill processes or preempt resources.
short_notes: Deadlock: 4 conditions must ALL hold simultaneously
RAG cycle with single instances = definite deadlock
Prevention: deny one of the 4 conditions
Banker's: ensures system stays in safe state
Safe state: sequence exists where all processes can complete
Recovery: process termination or resource preemption

expected_questions: Trace Banker's Algorithm for a given state to determine if it is safe.
Draw and interpret a Resource Allocation Graph with a cycle.
What are the four necessary conditions for deadlock? How can each be prevented?
Explain deadlock detection and recovery strategies.

videos.title: Page Replacement Algorithms — FCFS, LRU, Optimal

modules.title: Memory Management and File Systems
topics: Page Replacement Algorithms: FIFO, Optimal, LRU

notes.detailed: Paging divides physical memory into frames and logical memory into pages of equal size. The page table maps page numbers to frame numbers; logical address = (page no., offset). Address translation: physical address = frame_no × page_size + offset. Virtual memory allows processes to exceed physical memory size using disk as an extension. Demand paging loads pages only when needed; page fault triggers disk read. Page replacement algorithms: FIFO (evict oldest — simple, Belady's anomaly), Optimal (evict page used farthest in future — theoretical), LRU (evict least recently used — best practical, no Belady's anomaly). Thrashing: CPU spends more time paging than executing.
short_notes: Paging: eliminates external fragmentation
Page table: maps page → frame; stored in memory
TLB: cache for page table entries, reduces access time
Demand paging: load page only on page fault
FIFO: oldest page evicted, Belady's anomaly possible
Optimal: best performance, not implementable in practice
LRU: best practical, no Belady's anomaly
Thrashing: too many page faults, CPU utilization drops

important_topics: Page replacement algorithms trace and comparison
expected_questions: For the given reference string, calculate page faults using FCFS, Optimal, and LRU.
Explain logical to physical address translation in paging with an example.
What is thrashing? How can it be prevented?
Compare internal and external fragmentation. Which does paging eliminate?
Explain the different file allocation methods on disk.

most_asked_topics.topic: CPU Scheduling (Gantt chart + avg waiting time)

modules.title: Introduction and Regular Languages
topics: Equivalence of DFA and NFA — Subset Construction

notes.detailed: An alphabet Σ is a finite set of symbols. A string is a finite sequence over Σ. A language is any set of strings over Σ. A DFA M = (Q, Σ, δ, q0, F) where Q = states, δ = transition function, q0 = initial state, F = final states. DFA accepts a string w if processing it leads to a final state. An NFA differs in that δ: Q × Σ → 2^Q (can go to multiple states). NFA with ε-transitions can change state without reading input. Subset construction converts NFA to DFA by tracking sets of possible NFA states. A Regular Grammar has productions A → aB or A → a; it generates regular languages.
short_notes: DFA: exactly one transition per symbol per state
NFA: zero or more transitions per symbol per state
ε-NFA: can transition without reading input
Subset construction: states of DFA = subsets of NFA states
Regular Grammar: A → aB (right-linear) or A → Ba (left-linear)
DFA ≡ NFA ≡ ε-NFA ≡ Regular Grammar (all accept regular languages)

expected_questions: Design a DFA for a given language (e.g., strings ending in 'b', binary multiples of 5).
Convert a given NFA to an equivalent DFA using subset construction.
Write a Regular Grammar for a given language.
Draw an NFA for a given language and obtain the equivalent DFA.

modules.title: More on Regular Languages
topics: Conversion of RE to NFA (Thompson's Construction)

notes.detailed: A Regular Expression is defined recursively: ∅, ε, a (for a∈Σ) are RE; if r, s are RE then (r+s), (r·s), r* are RE. RE denotes the same class as DFA/NFA. Pumping Lemma: for every regular language L, ∃ n such that every string w∈L with |w|≥n can be written as xyz where |xy|≤n, |y|≥1, and xy^iz∈L for all i≥0. Used to prove languages are NOT regular (by contradiction). DFA minimization: merge equivalent states (indistinguishable states). Table-filling algorithm marks pairs of states as distinguishable starting from (final, non-final) pairs.
short_notes: RE operators: union (+), concatenation (·), Kleene star (*)
Thompson's construction: RE → NFA with ε-transitions
Kleene's construction: NFA → RE by state elimination
Pumping Lemma: use to DISPROVE a language is regular
Closure: regular languages closed under ∪, ∩, complement, concatenation, *
DFA minimization: merge indistinguishable states

expected_questions: Using the Pumping Lemma, prove that L = {aⁿbⁿ | n≥0} is not regular.
Write a Regular Expression for a given language and convert it to an NFA.
Using Kleene's construction, obtain the RE for a given NFA.
Obtain the minimum-state DFA for a given DFA.

modules.title: Myhill-Nerode Relations and Context-Free Grammars

notes.detailed: The Myhill-Nerode relation: x ≡_L y if for every z, xz∈L iff yz∈L. MNT states: L is regular iff ≡_L has finite index (finite number of equivalence classes). The number of classes equals the number of states in the minimum DFA. A CFG G = (V, Σ, R, S) generates strings via derivations. Ambiguity: a grammar is ambiguous if some string has two distinct parse trees (leftmost derivations). CNF: all productions A→BC or A→a — useful for CYK parsing. GNF: all productions A→aα where a∈Σ — useful for PDA construction.
short_notes: MNR: x≡y if they're indistinguishable by future strings
MNT: L regular ⟺ ≡_L has finite index
CFG: generates context-free languages (more powerful than regular)
Ambiguity: string has 2+ distinct leftmost derivations
CNF: A→BC or A→a (no ε except S→ε if ε∈L)
GNF: A→aα — terminal always first on RHS

important_topics: Converting CFG to CNF and GNF
expected_questions: Convert a given CFG to Chomsky Normal Form.
Convert a given CFG to Greibach Normal Form.
Show the equivalence classes of the Myhill-Nerode relation for a given language.
Explain ambiguity in CFG with an example. Is every ambiguous grammar's language inherently ambiguous?

videos.title: Parse Trees and Ambiguity in CFG

modules.title: More on Context-Free Languages
topics: Acceptance by Final State and Empty Stack

notes.detailed: A PDA is an NFA augmented with a stack. Formally: M = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q0, Z0, F) where Γ = stack alphabet, Z0 = initial stack symbol. Transitions: (q, a, A) → (p, γ) means in state q, reading a, with A on top of stack, go to state p and push γ. A PDA accepts by final state OR by empty stack — both models are equivalent. Every CFG has an equivalent PDA: in state q, push the entire RHS of a production. Pumping Lemma for CFL: every long string in a CFL can be pumped in two places simultaneously (uvxyz decomposition).
short_notes: PDA = NFA + stack (LIFO)
Acceptance: by final state OR empty stack (equivalent)
DPDA: deterministic PDA — accepts all DCFLs (e.g., palindromes with center marker)
CFG → PDA: push RHS, match terminals on stack
CFL Pumping Lemma: w = uvxyz, |vxy|≤n, |vy|≥1, uv^ix y^iz∈L

expected_questions: Design a PDA for L = {aⁿbⁿ | n≥0} and trace it on a sample string.
Using CFL Pumping Lemma, prove that L = {ww | w∈{a,b}*} is not context-free.
Convert a given CFG to an equivalent PDA.
What are the closure properties of CFLs? Which operations is CFL NOT closed under?

videos.title: PDA — Acceptance by Final State and Empty Stack

modules.title: Context-Sensitive Languages and Turing Machines

notes.detailed: A CSG has productions αAβ → αγβ where |γ|≥1 (no string shrinks). LBA is a TM restricted to the input tape length. A Turing Machine M = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q0, B, F) reads/writes on an infinite tape and moves left/right. TMs can compute any computable function (Church-Turing Thesis). The Halting Problem asks if a TM halts on a given input — proven undecidable by diagonalization. Chomsky Hierarchy: Type 3 (Regular, DFA) ⊂ Type 2 (CFL, PDA) ⊂ Type 1 (CSL, LBA) ⊂ Type 0 (RE, TM).
short_notes: CSG: productions never reduce string length
LBA: TM bounded to input length
TM: tape + read-write head, most powerful model
Church-Turing Thesis: TM = intuitive notion of algorithm
Halting Problem: undecidable (proven by diagonalization/contradiction)
Chomsky: Type 3 ⊂ Type 2 ⊂ Type 1 ⊂ Type 0
Recursive: decidable (TM always halts); RE: semi-decidable

expected_questions: Design a Turing Machine to compute the sum of two unary numbers.
Write a Context-Sensitive Grammar for L = {aⁿbⁿcⁿ | n≥0}.
Explain the Chomsky classification of formal languages.
Prove that the Halting Problem is undecidable.
Differentiate between Recursive and Recursively Enumerable Languages.

modules.title: Introduction and Physical Layer

notes.detailed: A computer network is an interconnected collection of autonomous computers. OSI model has 7 layers: Physical (bits), Data Link (frames), Network (packets), Transport (segments), Session, Presentation, Application. Each layer communicates with the layer above and below using well-defined interfaces. TCP/IP has 4 layers: Network Access, Internet, Transport, Application. Key differences: OSI is a reference model; TCP/IP is a practical implementation. Physical layer concerns: bandwidth (Hz, theoretical max), throughput (actual data rate), latency (propagation + transmission + queuing delay). Transmission media: guided (twisted pair, coaxial, fiber) and unguided (radio, microwave, infrared).
short_notes: OSI 7 layers: Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away (Physical-Data-Network-Transport-Session-Presentation-Application)
TCP/IP: 4 layers — Network Access, Internet, Transport, Application
Bandwidth: max frequency range (Hz)
Throughput: actual data rate (bps)
Latency = propagation + transmission + queuing delay
Fiber optic: highest bandwidth, immune to EMI, most expensive

expected_questions: Compare the OSI and TCP/IP reference models.
Explain the functions of each layer of the OSI model.
Calculate propagation time given distance and speed.
What are the characteristics of optical fiber as a transmission medium?

modules.title: Data Link Layer
topics: Sliding Window Protocols: Stop-and-Wait, Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat
Multiple Access Protocols: ALOHA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA

notes.detailed: The data link layer provides reliable node-to-node transfer. Framing delimits frames; error detection uses CRC (polynomial division — detect burst errors) and Hamming codes (detect and correct single-bit errors). Sliding window: Stop-and-Wait — send one frame, wait for ACK (inefficient). Go-Back-N — send up to W frames; on error, retransmit from error frame (window size ≤ 2^n-1). Selective Repeat — retransmit only erroneous frame (window size ≤ 2^(n-1)). Ethernet uses CSMA/CD: sense channel, transmit if idle, detect collision, jam and back-off (binary exponential backoff). 802.11 uses CSMA/CA: cannot detect collision over wireless, so avoids it.
short_notes: CRC: polynomial division, detects burst errors ≤ r bits
Hamming code: detect and correct single-bit errors
Stop-and-Wait: efficiency = 1/(1+2a) where a = propagation/transmission time
Go-Back-N: window ≤ 2ⁿ⁻¹, retransmit from error
Selective Repeat: window ≤ 2ⁿ⁻¹, retransmit only error frame
CSMA/CD: used in Ethernet (wired)
CSMA/CA: used in 802.11 (wireless), uses RTS/CTS

expected_questions: Compute the CRC for a given message using a given generator polynomial.
Compare Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat sliding window protocols.
Give the differences between CSMA/CD and CSMA/CA protocols.
Draw and explain the Ethernet frame format.
Why must Ethernet frames be at least 64 bytes? Justify.

videos.title: Stop and Wait, Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat

modules.title: Network Layer

notes.detailed: The network layer routes packets from source to destination across multiple networks. Routing algorithms can be static or dynamic. Dijkstra's shortest path: iteratively selects the nearest unvisited node. Distance Vector Routing (Bellman-Ford): each router knows cost to neighbors and shares its vector; vulnerable to count-to-infinity problem. Link State Routing: each router floods the entire network with its link states, then all routers compute shortest paths independently (used in OSPF). Congestion control prevents network overload: traffic shaping (token bucket, leaky bucket), admission control, traffic policing.
short_notes: Routing: static (fixed) or dynamic (adapts to network changes)
Dijkstra: greedy, finds shortest path, O(V²)
Distance Vector: Bellman-Ford, count-to-infinity problem
Link State: flood LSAs, build complete topology, run Dijkstra
Flooding: every packet to every neighbor — reliable but wasteful
Leaky bucket: smooth bursty traffic to constant rate
Token bucket: allows bursts up to bucket size

important_topics: Link state vs distance vector comparison
expected_questions: Given routing vectors from neighboring routers, compute the new routing table using distance vector routing.
Compare Distance Vector and Link State routing algorithms.
Explain the leaky bucket and token bucket congestion control techniques.
Apply Dijkstra's algorithm to find shortest paths in a given network.

videos.title: Congestion Control — Leaky Bucket and Token Bucket

modules.title: Network Layer in the Internet
topics: IP Protocol and IP Datagram Format

notes.detailed: IPv4 addresses are 32-bit, written in dotted decimal. Classes: A (0.0.0.0-127.255.255.255), B (128-191), C (192-223), D (multicast), E (research). Subnetting divides a network into subnets using a subnet mask. CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) uses prefix length notation (/24). ARP resolves IP to MAC address (broadcast). RARP: MAC to IP (legacy). DHCP dynamically assigns IP addresses. OSPF is a link-state intra-domain routing protocol. BGP is the inter-domain (internet backbone) routing protocol using path vectors. IPv6 has 128-bit addresses, simplified header, no fragmentation by routers.
short_notes: IPv4: 32 bits, 4 billion addresses (exhausted)
Subnetting: borrow host bits for network, use subnet mask
CIDR: /n notation, classless, aggregates routes
ARP: IP → MAC (broadcast query)
DHCP: dynamic IP assignment (DORA: Discover-Offer-Request-Ack)
OSPF: link-state, intra-domain, uses Dijkstra
BGP: path-vector, inter-domain, policy-based
IPv6: 128-bit, no fragmentation, built-in IPSec

expected_questions: Subnet a given Class C IP address into a given number of subnets. Find subnet mask and host range.
Compare IPv4 and IPv6.
Explain the DHCP address assignment process (DORA).
Compare OSPF and BGP routing protocols.
Explain the ARP process with a diagram.

modules.title: Transport Layer and Application Layer
topics: TCP Congestion Control: Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance

notes.detailed: UDP is connectionless, no reliability guarantee — used for DNS, streaming, VoIP (low overhead). TCP is connection-oriented, reliable, ordered, flow-controlled. TCP connection: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK (3-way handshake). Connection termination: FIN → FIN-ACK → FIN → ACK (4-way). TCP flow control: receiver advertises window size; sender cannot exceed it. TCP congestion control: slow start (doubles cwnd each RTT until ssthresh), congestion avoidance (linear increase), fast retransmit/recovery. DNS resolves domain names to IPs using a hierarchy of name servers. HTTP is stateless; HTTPS adds TLS.
short_notes: UDP: connectionless, no reliability, low overhead, fast
TCP: connection-oriented, reliable, ordered, flow+congestion controlled
3-way handshake: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK
TCP slow start: cwnd doubles each RTT until ssthresh
DNS hierarchy: Root → TLD → Authoritative
FTP: uses two connections (control:21, data:20)
SMTP: push protocol for email; POP3/IMAP: pull protocols

expected_questions: Explain the TCP three-way handshake for connection establishment.
Describe TCP congestion control with slow start and congestion avoidance.
Compare TCP and UDP with respect to reliability, overhead, and use cases.
Explain the DNS name resolution process.
Give the architecture of the World Wide Web.

videos.title: DNS — How Domain Name System Works
HTTP vs HTTPS and WWW Architecture

modules.title: Introduction to System Software
topics: SIC Instruction Set and Assembler Directives

notes.detailed: System software manages hardware and supports application software. Examples: OS, assemblers, compilers, linkers, loaders. SIC (Simplified Instructional Computer) is a hypothetical machine used for teaching. SIC has memory (32K bytes), registers (A, X, L, PC, SW), 24-bit instructions. SICXE adds index/base/PC-relative addressing, floating point registers, and larger memory.
short_notes: SIC: simplified architecture for teaching assembler concepts
SIC registers: A (accumulator), X (index), L (linkage), PC, SW
SICXE: extended SIC with more addressing modes and larger memory
Assembler directive: instructions to assembler, not machine code

expected_questions: Explain the architecture of SIC and SICXE.
What are assembler directives? Give examples.
Compare SIC and SICXE architectures.

modules.title: Assembly Language Programming and Assemblers
topics: Assembler Data Structures: SYMTAB, OPTAB, LOCCTR
Assembler Output: Header, Text, End Records

notes.detailed: An assembler converts assembly language to object code. A two-pass assembler: Pass 1 — assign addresses to all statements, build Symbol Table (SYMTAB). Pass 2 — generate object code using SYMTAB and Operation Table (OPTAB). LOCCTR tracks current address. Forward references (symbols used before definition) are resolved in pass 2. Object program format: Header (name, start, length), Text (code), End (first executable address) records.
short_notes: Pass 1: build SYMTAB, assign addresses
Pass 2: generate object code using SYMTAB and OPTAB
SYMTAB: symbol → address mapping
OPTAB: mnemonic → opcode mapping
LOCCTR: tracks current assembly address
Forward reference: symbol used before its definition

expected_questions: Explain the two-pass assembler with its data structures.
What is a forward reference? How is it handled?
Describe the format of an object program generated by an assembler.

videos.title: Object Program Format — Header, Text, End Records

modules.title: Assembler Design Options

notes.detailed: A one-pass assembler generates object code in a single scan. It handles forward references by backpatching — storing the symbol's use and filling the address when the definition is found. Load-and-go assembler loads object code directly into memory without storing it as a file — used for immediate execution. MASM (Microsoft Macro Assembler) is a real-world x86 assembler supporting complex macro facilities.
short_notes: One-pass assembler: handles forward refs by backpatching
Two-pass: most common, cleaner handling of forward refs
Load-and-go: no object file, directly loads into memory
Backpatching: fix-up addresses after symbol definition is found

expected_questions: Compare one-pass and two-pass assemblers.
Explain the backpatching technique used in one-pass assemblers.
What is a load-and-go assembler? Where is it used?

modules.title: Loaders and Linkers
topics: Loader Functions: Allocation, Linking, Relocation, Loading

notes.detailed: A loader loads object programs into memory for execution. Functions: allocation (assign memory), linking (combine modules), relocation (adjust addresses), loading (copy code). Absolute loader: no relocation needed; all addresses fixed. Relocating loader adjusts addresses at load time using modification records. Linking loader combines multiple object modules and performs relocation in a single pass. Dynamic linking (DLLs/shared libraries): library code linked at runtime, saves memory. Bootstrap loader is the first program run on power-on, loading the OS.
short_notes: Loader: allocation + linking + relocation + loading
Absolute loader: simplest, fixed addresses
Relocating loader: adjusts addresses using modification records
Dynamic linking: library loaded at runtime (DLL)
Bootstrap loader: first code run, loads the OS

expected_questions: Explain the functions of a loader.
Compare static and dynamic linking.
Describe the two-pass linking loader with data structures.
What is a bootstrap loader?

modules.title: Macro Preprocessors, Device Drivers, Text Editors, Debuggers
topics: Macro Processor Algorithm and Data Structures

notes.detailed: A macro is a shorthand for a sequence of instructions. Macro definition: MACRO...MEND. On invocation, the macro processor expands the macro by substituting parameters. Data structures: DEFTAB (definition table), NAMTAB (name table), ARGTAB (argument table). Nested macros: macros defined inside other macros (some processors don't allow). Conditional macros use IF/ELSE directives for conditional code generation. A device driver is software providing OS interface to a specific hardware device. A debugger allows step-through execution, breakpoints, and variable inspection.
short_notes: Macro: shorthand for code sequence, expanded at assembly time
DEFTAB: stores macro definitions
NAMTAB: maps macro names to DEFTAB entries
ARGTAB: stores actual arguments during expansion
Conditional macro: IF/ELSE directives in macro body
Debugger: breakpoints, single-step, watch variables

expected_questions: Explain the macro expansion process with DEFTAB, NAMTAB, ARGTAB.
Compare macros and subroutines.
Explain conditional macro expansion with an example.
What is a device driver? Explain its role.

videos.title: DEFTAB, NAMTAB, ARGTAB in Macro Processor

modules.title: Introduction and Lexical Analysis

notes.detailed: A compiler translates high-level source code to target code. Phases: Lexical Analysis → Syntax Analysis → Semantic Analysis → Intermediate Code Gen → Optimization → Code Generation. Analysis (front end) and Synthesis (back end). Lexical analyzer (scanner) reads source and produces tokens. A token is a (token-class, attribute) pair; a lexeme is the matched string; a pattern is the rule (regex). Example: for 'int x = 5', tokens are (keyword, int), (identifier, x), (op, =), (number, 5).
short_notes: Compiler phases: Lexical→Syntax→Semantic→ICG→Opt→CG
Lexical analysis: source → token stream
Token: (class, attribute) — e.g., (id, x)
Lexeme: actual matched string — e.g., 'velocity'
Pattern: regex defining token class
LEX: automatic scanner generator from regex specs

important_topics: Phases of compiler with symbol table and error handler
expected_questions: Explain all phases of a compiler with a running example.
Specify the analysis and synthesis parts of compilation.
Define token, lexeme, and pattern with examples.
Explain how finite automata are used in lexical analysis.

modules.title: Syntax Analysis

notes.detailed: A parser checks if the token stream matches the grammar. Top-down parsing builds parse tree from root. Recursive descent: one function per non-terminal. LL(1): Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, 1 lookahead. FIRST(α) = set of terminals that can begin strings derived from α. FOLLOW(A) = set of terminals that can appear after A. Predictive parsing table M[A,a]: M[A,a] = A→α if a∈FIRST(α). Left recursion (A → Aα | β) must be eliminated. Left factoring resolves common prefixes.
short_notes: LL(1): no left recursion, no ambiguity, uses lookahead
FIRST(α): terminals that can start strings from α; if ε derivable, include ε
FOLLOW(A): terminals that follow A in any sentential form
Left recursion: A→Aα|β becomes A→βA', A'→αA'|ε
Left factoring: A→αβ1|αβ2 becomes A→αA', A'→β1|β2

expected_questions: Compute FIRST and FOLLOW sets for a given grammar.
Construct the LL(1) predictive parsing table.
Eliminate left recursion from a given grammar.
Is the given grammar LL(1)? Justify.

modules.title: Bottom-Up Parsing
topics: LR Parsing: SLR, Canonical LR, LALR

notes.detailed: Bottom-up parsing reduces a string to the start symbol. Shift-reduce: either shift the next token onto the stack or reduce using a production. LR parsers are more powerful than LL. LR(0) items are productions with a dot marking current position. Closure: add items for all productions of non-terminals after the dot. GOTO: transition on symbol. SLR(1) uses FOLLOW sets to decide when to reduce. LALR(1) merges states with same core LR(1) items — most commonly used (used in YACC). Conflicts: shift-reduce and reduce-reduce.
short_notes: Bottom-up: rightmost derivation in reverse
LR(0) item: [A→α·β] — dot shows parser position
Closure: expand items when dot is before non-terminal
SLR: uses FOLLOW; simpler but less powerful
LALR: merge LR(1) states with same core — balanced power/space
YACC: automatic LALR(1) parser generator

important_topics: LR(0) items and SLR table construction
expected_questions: Construct the SLR parsing table for a given grammar.
Compute LR(0) items and GOTO function.
Explain shift-reduce and reduce-reduce conflicts with examples.
Compare SLR, LALR, and Canonical LR parsing.

modules.title: Semantic Analysis and Intermediate Code Generation

notes.detailed: Semantic analysis checks type compatibility and meaning correctness. Syntax-directed translation attaches attributes to grammar symbols. Synthesized attributes pass information up the parse tree; inherited attributes pass information down. S-attributed: only synthesized attributes (bottom-up evaluation). L-attributed: suitable for top-down (inherited from parent/left sibling). Three-address code: at most one operator per instruction (x = y op z). Quadruples: (op, arg1, arg2, result). Triples: (op, arg1, arg2) — result implicit from position.
short_notes: Synthesized attribute: computed from children
Inherited attribute: computed from parent/left siblings
Three-address code: x = y op z (max 1 operator)
Quadruple: (op, arg1, arg2, result) — 4-field entry
Triple: (op, arg1, arg2) — no explicit result field
L-attributed: can be evaluated in single left-to-right pass

expected_questions: Translate a given expression to three-address code and represent as quadruples.
Differentiate synthesized and inherited attributes with examples.
Explain S-attributed and L-attributed grammars.
Generate three-address code for a given if-else statement.

modules.title: Code Optimization and Code Generation
topics: Loop Optimization: Code Motion, Strength Reduction

notes.detailed: Code optimization improves generated code for speed or size without changing semantics. Machine-independent optimizations: common subexpression elimination, dead code elimination, constant folding. Loop optimization: code motion (move loop-invariant code outside), strength reduction (replace expensive ops with cheaper ones: x*2 → x<<1). Peephole optimization: local transformations on a small window of instructions — remove redundant loads/stores, simplify jumps. Register allocation: assign variables to registers; graph coloring approach. A basic block is a maximal sequence with one entry and one exit.
short_notes: Basic block: single entry, single exit, no branches inside
Common subexpression elimination: avoid recomputing same expression
Dead code elimination: remove unreachable/useless code
Loop invariant code motion: move constant computations outside loop
Strength reduction: x*2 → x<<1, x*x inside loop → increment
Peephole: local window optimization — remove redundant moves

expected_questions: What is the role of peephole optimization? Give examples.
Explain loop optimization techniques: code motion and strength reduction.
Identify basic blocks and draw the flow graph for a given code.
What are the issues in the design of a code generator?

videos.title: Loop Optimization — Code Motion and Strength Reduction

modules.title: Introduction to Algorithm Analysis

notes.detailed: An algorithm's efficiency is measured by time (steps) and space (memory) as functions of input size n. Asymptotic notation: Big-O (upper), Omega (lower), Theta (tight). Master Theorem solves T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n): Case 1: f(n) = O(n^(log_b a - ε)) → T = Θ(n^(log_b a)); Case 2: f(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a)) → T = Θ(n^(log_b a) log n); Case 3: f(n) = Ω(n^(log_b a + ε)) → T = Θ(f(n)).
short_notes: T(n) for merge sort: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n → O(n log n)
Master Theorem: 3 cases based on f(n) vs n^(log_b a)
Recurrence: substitute (iteration) or tree method
Amortized analysis: average cost over a sequence of operations

expected_questions: Solve T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n using the Master Theorem.
Calculate the time complexity of a given code segment.
Explain best, average, and worst-case complexity with examples.

videos.title: Algorithm Analysis — Time and Space Complexity

modules.title: Advanced Data Structures and Graph Algorithms

notes.detailed: Red-Black Trees are balanced BSTs with 5 properties ensuring O(log n) height. B-Trees are balanced multi-way trees used in databases/file systems for disk-based storage. Topological sort orders vertices of a DAG so all edges go from left to right — uses DFS (output nodes in reverse finish order) or Kahn's algorithm (BFS-based, remove sources). Strongly Connected Components (Kosaraju's algorithm): DFS, transpose graph, DFS on transpose in reverse finish order.
short_notes: Red-Black Tree: balanced BST, O(log n) all ops
B-Tree: multi-way balanced tree for disk storage
Topological sort: only for DAGs, multiple valid orderings
DFS on DAG: post-order reversed = topological order
SCC: Kosaraju's — 2 DFS passes on graph + transpose

expected_questions: Find the topological ordering of a given DAG.
Explain the properties of a Red-Black Tree.
Find strongly connected components of a given graph using Kosaraju's algorithm.
Perform BFS and DFS on a given graph and analyse time complexity.

modules.title: Divide and Conquer and Greedy Method

notes.detailed: Divide and Conquer: divide problem, conquer subproblems recursively, combine results. Merge Sort: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n = O(n log n). Strassen's: matrix multiplication in O(n^2.807) vs standard O(n^3). Greedy: makes locally optimal choice at each step. Fractional Knapsack: sort by value/weight ratio, fill greedily — O(n log n). Huffman Coding: optimal prefix codes using a min-heap — builds a tree where frequent symbols have shorter codes. Kruskal's MST: sort edges by weight, add if no cycle (uses Union-Find) — O(E log E). Prim's: grow tree from a vertex — O(V² or E log V with heap). Dijkstra's: greedy shortest path — O((V+E) log V).
short_notes: Merge Sort: O(n log n), stable, O(n) space
Quick Sort: O(n log n) avg, O(n²) worst, in-place
Strassen: O(n^2.807) matrix multiply
Fractional Knapsack: greedy by ratio — O(n log n)
Huffman: optimal prefix code, min-heap based
Kruskal: MST by edge sorting + union-find
Prim: MST by growing from source
Dijkstra: SSSP, no negative edges, O((V+E) log V)

expected_questions: State the Fractional Knapsack problem and write the greedy algorithm.
Construct a Huffman tree for given symbol frequencies.
Apply Kruskal's/Prim's algorithm to find the MST of a given graph.
Apply Dijkstra's algorithm for single source shortest path.
Trace Strassen's algorithm for 2×2 matrices.

videos.title: Divide and Conquer — Merge Sort and Quick Sort

modules.title: Dynamic Programming and Backtracking

notes.detailed: Dynamic Programming solves problems by combining solutions to overlapping subproblems (memoization or tabulation). Principle of Optimality: optimal solution contains optimal sub-solutions. 0/1 Knapsack: DP table dp[i][w] = max value using items 1..i with weight ≤ w — O(nW). Floyd-Warshall: all-pairs shortest paths — O(V³), handles negative edges (no negative cycles). Matrix Chain Multiplication: find optimal parenthesization — O(n³). LCS: length of longest common subsequence — O(mn). Backtracking: systematically explores solution space; prunes dead ends. N-Queens: place N queens on N×N board such that no two attack each other.
short_notes: DP: overlapping subproblems + optimal substructure
0/1 Knapsack: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], val[i]+dp[i-1][w-wt[i]])
Floyd-Warshall: dp[k][i][j] = min(dp[k-1][i][j], dp[k-1][i][k]+dp[k-1][k][j])
LCS: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]+1 if match, else max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
Backtracking: build solution incrementally, prune when constraint violated
N-Queens: O(n!) backtracking, check row/col/diagonal conflicts

expected_questions: Trace the DP algorithm for 0/1 Knapsack with given items and capacity.
Apply Floyd-Warshall algorithm to find all-pairs shortest paths.
List the steps of DP approach. Solve Matrix Chain Multiplication for given dimensions.
Trace the N-Queens problem using backtracking for n=4.
Find the LCS of two given strings using DP.

modules.title: Introduction to Complexity Theory
topics: Approximation Algorithms: Bin Packing, Graph Coloring
Randomized Algorithms: Monte Carlo, Las Vegas

notes.detailed: P: problems solvable in polynomial time. NP: problems verifiable in polynomial time. P ⊆ NP. NP-Hard: at least as hard as hardest NP problems. NP-Complete: NP-Hard ∩ NP. Cook's Theorem: SAT is NP-Complete (first NP-complete problem). Reductions: to prove X is NP-complete, reduce a known NP-complete problem to X in polynomial time. Clique: find a complete subgraph of size k — NP-complete. Approximation algorithms give solutions within a provable factor of optimal. Randomized algorithms use random choices: Monte Carlo (always fast, may be wrong), Las Vegas (always correct, expected fast).
short_notes: P: deterministic polynomial time
NP: non-deterministic polynomial time (or: verifiable in poly time)
NP-Complete: NP ∩ NP-Hard
Cook's Theorem: SAT is first NP-complete problem
Reduction: prove NP-hardness by reducing known NPC to new problem
Monte Carlo: may give wrong answer with low probability
Las Vegas: always correct, random running time

expected_questions: Differentiate between P and NP problems.
Define NP-Hard and NP-Complete. How do you prove a problem is NP-Complete?
Prove that the Clique problem is NP-Complete.
Explain Monte Carlo and Las Vegas randomized algorithms with examples.
What is an approximation algorithm? Explain with an example.

videos.title: Randomized Algorithms — Monte Carlo and Las Vegas

most_asked_topics.topic: P, NP, NP-Complete definitions and proof

modules.title: Introduction to AI and Intelligent Agents
topics: What is AI? — Definitions and Approaches
Agent Types: Simple Reflex, Model-Based, Goal-Based, Utility-Based, Learning

notes.detailed: AI is the science of making machines that think and act rationally. Four approaches: thinking humanly, acting humanly (Turing Test), thinking rationally, acting rationally. An agent is anything that perceives its environment via sensors and acts via actuators. PEAS: Performance measure, Environment, Actuators, Sensors. Rational agent: maximizes expected performance. Environment types: fully/partially observable, deterministic/stochastic, episodic/sequential, static/dynamic, discrete/continuous. Simple reflex agents use condition-action rules. Model-based agents maintain internal state. Goal-based agents reason about future. Utility-based agents maximize a utility function. Learning agents improve from experience.
short_notes: Turing Test: machine indistinguishable from human in text chat
Rational agent: best action for maximum expected performance
PEAS: Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors
Simple reflex: IF condition THEN action — no memory
Model-based: maintains internal model of world
Utility-based: maximizes utility function, handles trade-offs
Learning agent: critic + learning element + performance element + problem generator

expected_questions: Give the PEAS description for a given agent (e.g., automated taxi, medical diagnosis system).
Compare the five types of intelligent agents.
What is a rational agent? How does rationality differ from omniscience?
Explain the structure of a learning agent.

modules.title: Problem Solving by Searching
topics: Problem Formulation: State, Actions, Goal, Path Cost
Uninformed Search: BFS, DFS, Uniform Cost Search, Depth-Limited, Iterative Deepening
Informed Search: Greedy Best-First Search, A* Search
Local Search: Hill Climbing, Simulated Annealing, Genetic Algorithms

notes.detailed: A search problem: initial state, actions, transition model, goal test, path cost. Uninformed (blind) search has no domain knowledge. BFS: complete, optimal (unit cost), O(b^d) time/space. DFS: not complete (infinite spaces), not optimal, O(bm) space. Uniform Cost Search: expands lowest path cost — optimal, complete. Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS): combines DFS space efficiency with BFS completeness. Informed search uses heuristics. A* with admissible heuristic is optimal and complete. Admissible: h(n) ≤ true cost. Consistent (monotone): h(n) ≤ c(n,a,n') + h(n'). Hill Climbing: greedy local search, may get stuck at local maxima. Simulated Annealing: accepts worse solutions with decreasing probability.
short_notes: BFS: complete, optimal (unit cost), O(b^d) memory
DFS: O(bm) memory, not complete/optimal in general
A*: optimal if h is admissible, complete
Admissible heuristic: never overestimates true cost
Consistent heuristic: h(n) ≤ step cost + h(n')
Hill climbing: fast, no memory, stuck at local maxima
Simulated annealing: probabilistically accepts bad moves to escape local optima

important_topics: BFS vs DFS vs IDDFS comparison
expected_questions: Explain Best First Search and A* Search with examples.
Compare BFS, DFS, UCS, and A* on completeness, optimality, time, and space.
What are admissible and consistent heuristics? Give examples.
Explain hill climbing. What are its limitations?

modules.title: Problem Solving in Complex Environments

notes.detailed: Minimax algorithm: maximizer (MAX) maximizes score, minimizer (MIN) minimizes score. Search tree explored to terminal states. Optimal play assuming adversary plays optimally. Time: O(b^m), Space: O(bm). Alpha-Beta pruning eliminates branches that cannot affect the final decision, reducing search to O(b^(m/2)) in the best case. CSP: defined by variables, domains, and constraints. Backtracking search for CSP: assign values, check constraints, backtrack if violated. Heuristics: MRV (Minimum Remaining Values), degree heuristic, LCV (Least Constraining Value).
short_notes: Minimax: MAX picks max, MIN picks min, recursive
Alpha-β pruning: prune nodes that can't affect decision
Alpha: best option for MAX so far; Beta: best for MIN
CSP: variables + domains + constraints
MRV: choose variable with fewest legal values first
LCV: assign value that rules out fewest choices for neighbors

expected_questions: Trace the Minimax algorithm on a given game tree.
Explain alpha-beta pruning and trace it on an example.
Formulate a given problem as a CSP.
Explain backtracking search for CSP with MRV and LCV heuristics.

videos.title: Backtracking for CSP with MRV and LCV
Local Search — Hill Climbing and Variants

modules.title: Knowledge Representation and Reasoning
topics: First Order Predicate Logic (FOPL) — Syntax and Semantics

notes.detailed: A knowledge-based agent uses a knowledge base (KB) of sentences in a formal language. Propositional logic: variables, connectives (¬, ∧, ∨, →, ↔). Modus Ponens: from P and P→Q, infer Q. Resolution: the only inference rule needed for complete propositional reasoning. FOPL adds objects, predicates, functions, quantifiers (∀, ∃). Unification: finding substitutions to make expressions identical. Forward chaining (data-driven): start from known facts, apply rules, derive new facts. Backward chaining (goal-driven): start from goal, work backward to find supporting facts.
short_notes: Propositional logic: sentences about world using ∧,∨,¬,→,↔
FOPL: predicates, functions, quantifiers — richer representation
Modus Ponens: P, P→Q ⊢ Q
Resolution: negate goal, derive contradiction (proof by refutation)
Unification: θ such that SUBST(θ, α) = SUBST(θ, β)
Forward chaining: facts → rules → new facts (data-driven)
Backward chaining: goal → supporting conditions (goal-driven)

expected_questions: Represent given knowledge in First Order Predicate Logic.
Explain resolution as an inference rule. Prove a given statement using resolution.
Compare forward and backward chaining with examples.
Explain unification with an example.

modules.title: Machine Learning

notes.detailed: Learning from examples: inductive learning infers a general rule from specific examples. Decision tree learning: ID3/C4.5 uses Information Gain (entropy reduction) to select the best attribute at each node. Entropy H(S) = -Σ p_i log2(p_i). Information Gain IG(S, A) = H(S) - Σ|Sv|/|S| H(Sv). Overfitting: model too specific to training data — solved by pruning. Ensemble methods (bagging, boosting) combine multiple models. Reinforcement learning: agent learns by receiving rewards/penalties for actions in an environment.
short_notes: Supervised: labeled examples, learn input→output mapping
Decision tree: split on attribute with highest information gain
Entropy: H(S) = -Σ p_i log2(p_i) — measure of impurity
Information Gain: H(S) - weighted avg H of subsets
Overfitting: low training error, high test error — prune or regularize
Reinforcement learning: reward signal guides policy learning

expected_questions: Construct a decision tree for a given training dataset using ID3.
Calculate entropy and information gain for given data.
Explain supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning with examples.
What is overfitting? How can it be addressed?

videos.title: Supervised vs Unsupervised vs Reinforcement Learning
